documents.content:
# ABOUT_ANKI.md

Principal Engineer specializing in **AI-native orchestration systems**, `developer platforms`, and `runtime workflows`.

I have **20+ years** of experience building `scalable execution platforms` and developer tooling across **enterprise** and **embedded** environments, with recent focus on **AI-driven workflows** that combine `deterministic execution` with `adaptive LLM` capabilities.

## Current Focus

- `AI orchestration` — agentic workflows and deterministic execution paths
- `Developer platforms` — interactive authoring tools
- `Browser-native` and `local-first` AI systems
- `Retrieval workflows` — embeddings and function calling
- `TypeScript` platform architecture
- `Low-latency` interactive systems

## What I Build

I like building systems that turn **complex runtime behavior** into usable tools: `visual workflow editors`, **AI-assisted authoring**, `local-first guardrails`, `retrieval-based interactions`, and products where humans can **iteratively collaborate with AI** rather than rely on one-shot generation.

## Professional Throughline

From **`embedded JVM` runtimes** at **Sun Microsystems**, to **mobile and web application frameworks** at **Oracle**, to recent **independent AI systems** and developer tools — my work has consistently focused on `execution platforms`, `developer productivity`, and **interactive systems** that make complex technology usable.

# Resume

## Anki Nelaturu

**Santa Clara, CA**  
(408) 431-3061  
anki.r.nelaturu@gmail.com  
linkedin.com/in/ankinelaturu

### Principal AI Product Engineer | AI Orchestration, Agentic Workflows & Developer Platforms

Principal engineer specializing in AI-native orchestration systems, developer platforms, and runtime workflows. Over 20 years building scalable execution platforms, developer tooling, embedded runtimes, and enterprise application platforms. Recent focus includes AI-assisted engineering workflows, local-first AI systems, retrieval pipelines, semantic orchestration, and human-in-the-loop execution models.

---

## Skills

- AI orchestration workflows
- Agentic systems
- LLM APIs
- Function calling
- Retrieval workflows
- Embeddings
- TypeScript
- JavaScript (ES6+)
- React
- Next.js
- Node.js
- Runtime systems
- Asynchronous execution
- Execution pipelines
- WebSockets
- ONNX
- REST APIs
- CI/CD
- Babel
- Rollup
- Jasmine
- Karma

---

## Selected Projects

### Storybook Arcade (Once Upon a GPT)
https://youtu.be/ZOrma2xMTy8

Engineered a multimodal orchestration pipeline combining LLM reasoning, image generation, and text-to-speech systems into synchronized story-generation workflows with streaming execution and low-latency feedback.

---

## Professional Experience

### Independent Product Engineer — AI Systems & Developer Tools
**Oct 2025 – Present**

Built AI-driven systems across developer tooling, learning orchestration, and content workflows, emphasizing low-latency execution, streaming interactions, and iterative human-in-the-loop refinement.

#### OzMap
https://ozmap.app

Architected a graph-based learning orchestration system enabling dynamic topic generation, branching exploration paths, and synchronized execution state across interactive sessions.

#### Lintern
https://lintern.app

Built a local-first data inspection system for AI workflows using heuristic and embedding-based semantic detection pipelines to identify sensitive information with low-latency, in-browser execution.

#### Crafticle
https://crafticle.app

Built an AI-assisted content orchestration system enabling structured idea extraction, transformation pipelines, and iterative human-guided article composition.

---

### Oracle

#### Consulting Member of Technical Staff
**Sep 2024 – Sep 2025**

**Technical Lead – TypeScript Enablement & Generative AI Integration (Oracle Visual Builder)**

- Led end-to-end TypeScript enablement across runtime, build systems, and tooling.
- Built an LLM-driven orchestration engine using function-calling APIs to synthesize deterministic multi-step action sequences from natural language instructions.
- Built an on-the-fly Babel transpilation pipeline and integrated Jasmine/Karma testing into CI workflows.

#### Principal Member of Technical Staff
**Jul 2018 – Aug 2024**

**Technical Lead – Visual Builder Design-Time & Action Chain Framework**

- Led a team of 4–5 engineers delivering core Oracle Visual Builder platform features.
- Architected a runtime execution framework and dependency-driven orchestration graph for asynchronous workflows.
- Contributed across frontend and server-side components.
- Partnered with Product and UX teams to improve developer productivity.
- Established architectural guidelines and conducted code reviews.

#### Principal Member of Technical Staff
**Dec 2013 – Jul 2018**

**Architect – Oracle Mobile Application Framework (MAF)**

- Contributed to architecture and development of a cross-platform mobile framework supporting iOS, Android, and Windows.
- Led migration to Java 8 and modern runtime integrations.
- Designed runtime components across Java, C/C++, and native mobile layers.
- Presented at JavaOne and Oracle OpenWorld.

#### Principal Member of Technical Staff
**Feb 2011 – Dec 2013**

- Contributed to Java Embedded Suite (JES).
- Designed and developed REST services and runtime components for embedded and distributed IoT environments.

---

### Sun Microsystems

#### Principal Member of Technical Staff
**Jan 2004 – Feb 2011**

- Contributed to resource-constrained JVM runtime systems.
- Focused on synchronization, execution performance, portability, and secure embedded execution.
- Delivered technical sessions and workshops at JavaOne.

---

## Education

### Master of Science, Computer Science
**1999**

National Institute of Electronics & Information Technology (NIELIT), India

---

## Patents

- US10198279B2 — Thread synchronization for platform neutrality
- US8978023 — Virtual channel for embedded process communication
- US8863126 — Canonical mechanism for securely assembling features into a mobile application
- US8856805 — Java virtual machine embedded in a native mobile application

documents.content:
# ai-orchestration.md

## How I Think About AI Orchestration

AI orchestration is not just chaining prompts. The interesting work is connecting adaptive LLM capabilities with deterministic, inspectable, recoverable execution models.

## Relevant Work

- LLM-driven action sequence generation in Oracle Visual Builder
- ZeroFabric runtime capability coordination concept
- Storybook Arcade multimodal orchestration pipeline
- Crafticle structured-to-freeform transformation pipeline
- Lintern local inspection pipelines for AI workflows

## Core Themes

- Function calling
- Structured outputs
- Retrieval workflows
- Human-in-the-loop refinement
- Runtime constraints
- Deterministic execution boundaries

documents.content:
# browser-runtimes.md

## Browser Runtime Interests

I am interested in the browser as an increasingly capable AI runtime: local inference, semantic matching, privacy-preserving inspection, DOM-aware tools, and interactive applications that run close to the user.

## Relevant Work

- Lintern Chrome extension and local-first detection model
- Ask Rudy / Rudy Finds browser-oriented retrieval experiments
- ONNX and local AI experimentation
- TypeScript-heavy interactive systems

## Why It Matters

Not every AI workflow should require sending user data to a remote service. Browser-native AI creates opportunities for privacy, low-latency feedback, and context-aware interaction.

documents.content:
# developer-platforms.md

## Developer Platform Experience

Much of my career has focused on developer platforms and tools that make complex systems easier to build, inspect, and maintain.

## Relevant Systems

- Oracle Visual Builder
- Action Chain Editor
- Oracle Mobile Application Framework
- TypeScript enablement across runtime/build/tooling layers
- AI-assisted workflow generation

## Design Principle

A strong developer platform should help users move faster without hiding the execution model completely. The best tools are approachable, but still inspectable and debuggable.

# interactive-systems.md

## Interactive Systems

I care about systems where UI is not just presentation. In my work, the interface often *is* the system: a visual programming canvas, an authoring environment, a retrieval surface, or a low-latency AI collaboration loop.

## Relevant Work

- Action Chain Editor
- Storybook Arcade
- OzMap learning trails
- Crafticle writing environment
- Lintern browser feedback loops

## Design Principle

The most valuable AI interfaces are not one-shot prompt boxes. They support iteration, inspection, correction, and continuous feedback.

documents.title: Aimless Dude & Hyper Dog
documents.summary: Daily illustrated diary with Caiku micro-poems and filmstrip viewer.
documents.content:
# Aimless Dude & Hyper Dog

A daily illustrated diary about two idiots exploring the world together.

Light. Cozy. Slightly chaotic. No productivity. No plans. Just life.

Each frame pairs a square illustration with a **Caiku** — a prehistoric micro-poem written in caveman-simple language.

Images are expected in `/images/adhd/` and named `d0.png`, `d1.png`, `d2.png`, ... `d125.png`.


---

# 🪨 PROLOGUE — Journey Begins

## Day 0

![Day 0](/images/adhd/d0.png)

Journey begins.
Rudy ready.
I ????.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog


---

# 🌿 Chapter 1 — Enjoying the World

## Day 1

![Day 1](/images/adhd/d1.png)

Big sky.
Rudy sniff everything.
Good day.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 2

![Day 2](/images/adhd/d2.png)

Played fetch.
Rudy never returned stick.
Negotiations failed.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 3

![Day 3](/images/adhd/d3.png)

Watched clouds.
Cloud look like dog.
Rudy proud.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 4

![Day 4](/images/adhd/d4.png)

Organized rocks by size.
Forgot why.
Rudy no care.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 5

![Day 5](/images/adhd/d5.png)

Found frog.
Rudy chased frog.
Frog undefeated.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 6

![Day 6](/images/adhd/d6.png)

Swam in river.
Rudy swam too.
Both heroic. Briefly.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 7

![Day 7](/images/adhd/d7.png)

Morning sun.
Rudy already awake.
I not ready.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog


---

# 🔥 Chapter 2 — Early Experiments

## Day 8

![Day 8](/images/adhd/d8.png)

Tried to invent spear.
Got distracted by shiny rock.
Still holding rock.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 9

![Day 9](/images/adhd/d9.png)

Rudy barked at tree.
Tree unmoved.
Rudy victorious.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 10

![Day 10](/images/adhd/d10.png)

Started building hut.
Now digging hole.
Why hole? Unknown.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 11

![Day 11](/images/adhd/d11.png)

Found fire by accident.
Celebrated.
Rudy stepped in it.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 12

![Day 12](/images/adhd/d12.png)

Cooked something.
Smelled like success.
Actually smoke.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 13

![Day 13](/images/adhd/d13.png)

Rudy chased bird.
Bird flew.
Rudy still running.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 14

![Day 14](/images/adhd/d14.png)

Made sharper stick.
Lost sharper stick.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 15

![Day 15](/images/adhd/d15.png)

Followed river.
River kept going.
Very rude.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog


---

# 🐕 Chapter 3 — Camp Life & Dog Logic

## Day 16

![Day 16](/images/adhd/d16.png)

Spent all day thinking.
Built nothing.
Good day.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 17

![Day 17](/images/adhd/d17.png)

Rudy suspicious of same rock as yesterday.
Still suspicious.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 18

![Day 18](/images/adhd/d18.png)

Stacked stones carefully.
Wind disagreed.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 19

![Day 19](/images/adhd/d19.png)

Tried hunting.
Ended up collecting leaves.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 20

![Day 20](/images/adhd/d20.png)

Rudy found stick.
Would not share.
Negotiations failed.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 21

![Day 21](/images/adhd/d21.png)

Redid yesterday’s work.
Looks exactly same.

#AimlessDudeHyperDog

## Day 22

chunks.text:
Title: Aimless Dude & Hyper Dog
Path: creative-systems/aimless-dude-hyper-dog.md
Section: Day

A daily illustrated diary about two idiots exploring the world together.

Light. Cozy. Slightly chaotic. No productivity. No plans. Just life.

Each frame pairs a square illustration with a **Caiku** — a prehistoric micro-poem written in caveman-simple language.

Images are expected in `/images/adhd/` and named `d0.png`, `d1.png`, `d2.png`, ... `d125.png`.

documents.title: ChatGPT Export Summary
documents.summary: Analysis of my ChatGPT usage patterns, conversations, and token consumption over time.
documents.elevatorPitch: A data-driven look at how I have used ChatGPT as a thinking partner, coding assistant, research tool, and idea exploration system.
documents.content:
# ChatGPT Export Summary


## What It Is

This project is a personal analysis of my ChatGPT usage history based on a full export of my conversations.

What started as simple curiosity quickly became an interesting data exploration exercise. I wanted to understand how deeply AI had become integrated into my daily workflow and whether the numbers would reveal patterns that I had not noticed myself.

The resulting dataset contains more than 1,200 conversations, nearly 50,000 messages, and over 17 million estimated tokens exchanged across project work, software engineering discussions, product ideation, research, education, creative writing, career exploration, and everyday problem solving.

Rather than treating ChatGPT as a search engine replacement, the data shows how I increasingly used it as a thinking partner, design reviewer, coding assistant, research companion, brainstorming tool, and project collaborator.

```analytics
{
  "type": "bubble-metrics",
  "title": "ChatGPT Usage Snapshot [Jun 02, 2026]",
  "scale": "sqrt",

    { "label": "Tokens", "value": 17116709, "display": "17.1M", "emphasis": "primary" },
    { "label": "Assistant Tokens", "value": 14409662, "display": "14.4M" },
    { "label": "User Tokens", "value": 2707047, "display": "2.7M" },
    { "label": "Messages", "value": 48525, "display": "48.5K" },
    { "label": "Chats", "value": 1231, "display": "1,231" }
  ]
}
```
```analytics
{
  "type": "pie-chart",
  "title": "ChatGPT Tokens by Year [Jun 02, 2026]",
  "items": [
    { "label": "2023", "value": 576348, "display": "576K" },
    { "label": "2024", "value": 2390255, "display": "2.4M" },
    { "label": "2025", "value": 6723370, "display": "6.7M" },
    { "label": "2026", "value": 7416172, "display": "7.4M", "emphasis": "primary" }
  ]
}
```
## Why I Built It

After requesting my ChatGPT export, I initially expected a few interesting statistics.

Instead, I discovered a surprisingly detailed record of how my interests evolved over time.

The conversations captured the progression of dozens of projects, product ideas, technical explorations, job-search activities, educational content, AI experiments, and personal learning efforts.

I wanted to quantify that journey.

The project became an opportunity to explore questions such as:

- How much was I actually using ChatGPT?
- Which periods showed the highest activity?
- How conversational were the interactions?
- Was I primarily consuming answers or collaborating interactively?
- How had AI become integrated into my day-to-day work?

## Key Findings

- 1,231 conversations analyzed.
- 48,525 total messages exchanged.
- More than 17 million estimated tokens.
- Approximately 14.4 million assistant tokens.
- Approximately 2.7 million user tokens.
- Nearly 40 messages per conversation on average.
- AI became embedded in software engineering, product development, research, writing, learning, and planning workflows.

One surprising observation was that the average conversation length was significantly longer than expected. Many discussions evolved into extended collaborative sessions rather than simple question-and-answer exchanges.

## Technical Highlights

- Python-based export processing.
- Large-scale JSON conversation analysis.
- Token estimation workflows.
- CSV generation and reporting.
- Statistical aggregation across thousands of messages.
- Trend analysis over time.
- Structured extraction of conversation metadata.
- Visualization-ready datasets.

## Key Features

- Analyze complete ChatGPT exports.
- Calculate conversation-level statistics.
- Estimate token usage.
- Generate monthly and aggregate reports.
- Explore long-term usage patterns.
- Identify trends across conversations.
- Produce visualization-friendly output.
- Support personal AI workflow analysis.

## Technical Challenges

The export format contains deeply nested conversation structures that evolved over time.

Building reliable analysis required handling different message formats, extracting conversation trees, normalizing data, estimating token counts, and generating meaningful aggregate statistics.

Another challenge was interpretation.

Large numbers are interesting, but the real value comes from understanding what those numbers represent. The project attempts to connect usage metrics to actual workflows and habits rather than presenting raw statistics in isolation.

## Why It Matters

This analysis provides a unique perspective on how AI has become part of my working process.

The numbers themselves are less interesting than what they represent.

The dataset captures years of experimentation, learning, building, researching, writing, planning, and problem solving. It documents how AI evolved from an occasional tool into a regular collaborator across many different activities.

For me, the project serves as both a personal reflection and a practical example of using data analysis to better understand how emerging tools influence day-to-day work.

## Totals

| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Conversations | 1,231 |
| Total messages | 48,525 |
| User messages | 24,421 |
| Assistant messages | 24,104 |
| Total characters | 68,537,975 |
| Estimated user tokens | 2,707,047 |
| Estimated assistant tokens | 14,409,662 |
| Estimated total tokens | 17,116,709 |
| Avg messages / conversation | 39.4 |
| Avg tokens / conversation | 13,905 |

## Notes

Token counts are estimates derived from exported content and should not be interpreted as OpenAI billing or internal usage figures.

Actual token accounting may differ because ChatGPT applies model-specific tokenization, system prompts, hidden context, and internal processing that are not included in the exported data.

documents.content:
# Java Embedded Suite

## What It Is

Java Embedded Suite was focused on enabling Java-based services and runtime components in embedded and distributed environments.

The work connected Java runtime systems, REST services, and early IoT-style distributed environments.

## Time Period

**Feb 2011 - Dec 2013**

## Role

**Principal Member of Technical Staff, Oracle**

## What I Did

I contributed to Java Embedded Suite by designing and developing RESTful services and runtime components for embedded and distributed IoT environments.

The work involved building infrastructure that allowed constrained or embedded systems to expose capabilities, communicate with other systems, and participate in distributed architectures.

## Technical Highlights

- Designed and developed RESTful services.
- Built runtime components for embedded environments.
- Worked on distributed IoT-style system components.
- Contributed to Java-based embedded infrastructure.
- Focused on reliability and runtime constraints.

## Key Features / Outcomes

- Supported service-oriented interaction patterns for embedded systems.
- Improved communication capabilities across distributed environments.
- Contributed to runtime infrastructure for embedded Java use cases.
- Helped bridge embedded systems and enterprise service patterns.

## Technical Challenges

Embedded and distributed environments bring constraints around resources, reliability, connectivity, and runtime footprint.

The system needed to expose useful services while remaining practical for embedded deployment scenarios.

## Why It Matters

This experience connects my earlier embedded runtime background with later work in platforms, workflows, and web application systems.

It also contributed to my long-running interest in making constrained runtime environments useful and programmable.

documents.content:
# Oracle Mobile Application Framework

## What It Is

Oracle Mobile Application Framework, or MAF, was a cross-platform mobile framework for building enterprise mobile applications across iOS, Android, and Windows platforms.

The work involved framework architecture, runtime components, Java integration, native mobile layers, and cross-platform reliability.

## Time Period

**Dec 2013 - Jul 2018**

## Role

**Principal Member of Technical Staff, Oracle**

**Architect - Oracle Mobile Application Framework (MAF)**

## What I Did

I contributed to the architecture and development of a cross-platform mobile framework supporting iOS, Android, and Windows platforms.

I led migration efforts to Java 8 and modern runtime integrations, and designed runtime components across Java, C/C++, and native mobile layers.

The work focused on performance optimization, runtime integration, cross-platform reliability, and enterprise mobile application support.

## Technical Highlights

- Contributed to architecture of a cross-platform mobile framework.
- Supported iOS, Android, and Windows platforms.
- Led migration to Java 8.
- Designed runtime components across Java, C/C++, and native mobile layers.
- Focused on performance optimization and runtime reliability.
- Worked across embedded runtime and mobile application boundaries.
- Delivered technical sessions at JavaOne and Oracle OpenWorld.

## Key Features / Outcomes

- Helped enable enterprise mobile applications across multiple platforms.
- Modernized runtime support through Java 8 migration.
- Improved cross-platform reliability and runtime integration.
- Supported native mobile interoperability.
- Contributed to a framework used by enterprise developers building mobile applications.

## Technical Challenges

Cross-platform mobile frameworks must hide platform differences without eliminating the unique constraints of each operating system.

The framework needed to support enterprise-grade applications while dealing with runtime integration, native mobile APIs, Java execution, performance constraints, and differences across iOS, Android, and Windows.

## Why It Matters

This experience deepened my understanding of runtime systems, platform abstraction, and cross-platform developer tooling.

It also connects to my later browser-native and local-first AI work. In both cases, the challenge is similar: make complex runtime capabilities available through a developer-friendly platform.

documents.title: TypeScript Enablement & Generative AI Integration
documents.summary: Modernized Oracle Visual Builder with TypeScript enablement and LLM-driven action orchestration.
documents.elevatorPitch: Led TypeScript modernization and built an LLM-driven orchestration engine for deterministic multi-step action generation.
documents.content:
# TypeScript Enablement & Generative AI Integration

## What It Is

This was my most recent Oracle role, focused on modernizing Oracle Visual Builder with TypeScript support and integrating generative AI into design-time developer workflows.

The work combined traditional platform engineering with emerging AI-assisted development patterns. The goal was to improve developer productivity, modernize the runtime and tooling pipeline, and explore how natural language instructions could synthesize deterministic multi-step application behavior.

## Time Period

**Sep 2024 - Sep 2025**

## Role

**Consulting Member of Technical Staff, Oracle**

**Technical Lead - TypeScript Enablement & Generative AI Integration (Oracle Visual Builder)**

## What I Did

I led end-to-end TypeScript enablement across runtime, build systems, and tooling for Oracle Visual Builder.

I also built an LLM-driven orchestration engine using function-calling APIs to synthesize deterministic multi-step action sequences from natural language instructions.

This work sat at the intersection of developer platforms, AI-assisted tooling, and execution workflows.

## Technical Highlights

- Led TypeScript enablement across runtime, build systems, and tooling.
- Modernized enterprise-scale web application framework capabilities.
- Built an LLM-driven orchestration engine using function-calling APIs.
- Synthesized deterministic multi-step action sequences from natural language instructions.
- Built an on-the-fly Babel transpilation pipeline.
- Integrated Jasmine/Karma-based unit testing into CI workflows.
- Focused on stability, backward compatibility, and developer productivity.

## Key Features / Outcomes

- Improved developer experience for Visual Builder users.
- Added TypeScript-oriented modernization paths.
- Enabled AI-assisted action generation workflows.
- Connected natural language inputs to structured, executable action sequences.
- Strengthened automated testing and build-time validation.
- Helped move a mature enterprise platform toward AI-assisted development patterns.

## Technical Challenges

The challenge was not simply adding TypeScript syntax support. Visual Builder is an enterprise platform with existing runtime behavior, design-time expectations, backward compatibility constraints, and large customer usage patterns.

The AI integration also required discipline. The system could not allow an LLM to produce arbitrary behavior. It needed structured function-calling outputs that could map into deterministic action sequences and remain understandable to developers.

## Why It Matters

This role connects directly to my current AI product engineering focus.

It combined my long experience in enterprise developer platforms with newer AI-assisted engineering workflows. It also reinforced a pattern I continue to explore: LLMs are most useful in production systems when they are paired with deterministic execution models, validation, and clear boundaries.

The challenge was not simply adding TypeScript syntax support. Visual Builder is an enterprise platform with existing runtime behavior, design-time expectations, backward compatibility constraints, and large customer usage patterns.

The AI integration also required discipline. The system could not allow an LLM to produce arbitrary behavior. It needed structured function-calling outputs that could map into deterministic action sequences and remain understandable to developers.

documents.title: Visual Builder Design-Time & Action Chain Framework
documents.summary: Led core Oracle Visual Builder platform work including action chains, runtime execution, and design-time developer workflows.
documents.elevatorPitch: Led platform engineering for Visual Builder design-time systems and architected action-chain runtime orchestration.
documents.content:
# Visual Builder Design-Time & Action Chain Framework

## What It Is

This work centered on Oracle Visual Builder, a visual application development platform used to build web and enterprise applications.

My focus was on design-time platform capabilities and the Action Chain Framework, which provided a structured way to define, orchestrate, and execute application behavior.

## Time Period

**Jul 2018 - Aug 2024**

## Role

**Principal Member of Technical Staff, Oracle**

**Technical Lead - Visual Builder Design-Time & Action Chain Framework**

## What I Did

I led a team of 4-5 engineers designing and delivering core platform features for Oracle Visual Builder.

A major part of the work involved architecting a runtime execution framework and dependency-driven orchestration graph for asynchronous action workflows with deterministic state propagation and execution sequencing.

I contributed across frontend and server-side components, working closely with Product and UX teams to improve developer productivity and application consistency.

## Technical Highlights

- Led engineering delivery for core Visual Builder platform features.
- Architected runtime execution framework for action workflows.
- Designed dependency-driven orchestration graph for asynchronous execution.
- Supported deterministic state propagation and execution sequencing.
- Contributed across frontend layers using JavaScript, ES6, SCSS, and HTML.
- Worked on server-side components to improve performance and maintainability.
- Conducted code reviews and established architectural guidelines.
- Partnered with Product and UX teams to improve developer workflows.

## Key Features / Outcomes

- Improved the way application logic was modeled visually.
- Supported asynchronous workflow execution through action chains.
- Improved consistency and maintainability of application behavior.
- Helped make complex application behavior more understandable to developers.
- Established architecture patterns for scalable design-time platform development.
- Mentored and guided engineers through technical design and implementation.

## Technical Challenges

Visual development platforms have a difficult balance to maintain. They must simplify application development for users while still generating reliable, maintainable, and scalable application behavior underneath.

The Action Chain Framework needed to support complex asynchronous behavior without becoming unpredictable. Execution order, state propagation, dependency handling, error conditions, and developer comprehension all mattered.

## Why It Matters

This experience is central to my background in developer platforms.

It shaped how I think about visual development systems, runtime orchestration, low-code tooling, and the relationship between user-facing design tools and the execution models underneath them.

Many of my current AI projects build on the same ideas: structured workflows, deterministic execution, user-facing orchestration, and tools that help people build complex systems more easily.

documents.content:
# Embedded JVM Runtime Systems

## What It Is

This work focused on resource-constrained JVM runtime systems at Sun Microsystems.

The systems were designed for embedded environments where performance, portability, synchronization, and secure execution mattered deeply.

## Time Period

**Jan 2004 - Feb 2011**

## Role

**Principal Member of Technical Staff, Sun Microsystems**

## What I Did

I contributed to JVM runtime systems for constrained embedded environments.

The work focused on synchronization, execution performance, portability, and secure embedded execution. I also delivered technical sessions and workshops at JavaOne.

## Technical Highlights

- Contributed to resource-constrained JVM runtime systems.
- Worked on synchronization and platform neutrality.
- Focused on execution performance.
- Supported portability across embedded environments.
- Worked on secure embedded execution.
- Delivered technical sessions and workshops at JavaOne.

## Key Features / Outcomes

- Improved runtime behavior in constrained environments.
- Contributed to platform-neutral synchronization mechanisms.
- Supported portability and performance for embedded JVM scenarios.
- Helped make Java runtime technology viable in embedded contexts.

## Technical Challenges

Embedded JVM work requires balancing language runtime capabilities with resource limits.

Memory, performance, platform differences, synchronization behavior, and security all influence runtime design. Small implementation details can have significant impact when the environment is constrained.

## Why It Matters

This experience forms the foundation of my systems background.

It shaped how I think about runtimes, execution models, portability, performance, and the engineering tradeoffs underneath developer-facing platforms.

Those lessons continue to influence my later work in Visual Builder, mobile frameworks, browser runtimes, local AI, and AI orchestration systems.

documents.content:
# portfolio-workspace.md

## The Idea

This site is not intended to be a traditional portfolio. It is a professional knowledge workspace: markdown files, an IDE-like explorer, a terminal-style interface, and eventually a local AI assistant that answers questions from the same content used by the UI.

## Why Markdown

Markdown gives the site a clean content source. Add a new `.md` file and the workspace can automatically update the explorer, search index, and assistant corpus.

## Future AI Assistant

The next phase is local-first retrieval over this content, using browser-side embeddings and a local model such as Gemma/WebLLM where practical.

documents.title: Concurrency Systems
documents.summary: Thread synchronization and concurrent execution innovations.
documents.content:
# thread-synchronization.md

## Patent

**US10198279B2 — Thread synchronization for platform neutrality**

A system performs thread synchronization across layers of code that implement an application, including native code, system code, and code in a virtual machine (“VM”). The system makes a call by the native code to the system code; and sends a message by the system code to the code in the VM. The system then sends a first response by the code in the VM to the system code; and sends a second response by the system code to the native code, where each one of the native code, the system code, and the code in the VM implements wait and notify functionality for communication with other codes that implement the application.

documents.title: Ask Rudy
documents.summary: Conversational interface for guided exploration and grounded Q&A over local or page-level knowledge.
documents.elevatorPitch: Browser-native AI assistant that answers questions about the content in front of the user using retrieval and grounded context.
documents.content:
# Ask Rudy

> A conversational assistant for asking grounded questions about the content already in front of the user.

## What It Is

Ask Rudy explores a simple idea:

What if every page, document, note, or workspace could answer questions about itself?

Instead of acting as a general-purpose chatbot, Ask Rudy focuses on content-aware conversations. The assistant operates close to the information the user is currently viewing and attempts to answer questions using that context rather than relying entirely on model memory.

The project explores retrieval-based question answering experiences where users can interact with documents, articles, notes, saved content, browser pages, or personal knowledge collections through natural conversation.

Rather than asking users to manually search, skim, and piece together information, Ask Rudy attempts to act as a guide that helps users understand and navigate the content already available to them.

The goal is not to replace search. The goal is to make information easier to explore.

## Why I Built It

Many AI assistants require users to leave the content they are working with and move into a separate chat interface.

That workflow always felt backwards.

Users are often reading a page, researching a topic, studying material, reviewing documentation, or exploring information when a question appears. The assistant should be able to help directly within that context.

Ask Rudy was built to explore that idea.

I wanted to investigate what happens when AI becomes a companion to existing content instead of acting as an isolated destination.

The project also allowed me to explore several themes that repeatedly appear throughout my work:

- Retrieval-based AI systems
- Browser-native experiences
- Grounded answers
- Local-first AI concepts
- Explainable AI interactions
- Human-friendly knowledge exploration

## Technical Highlights

- Retrieval-based question answering.
- Grounded responses based on available content.
- Browser-native interaction patterns.
- Context-aware conversational workflows.
- Embedding-driven retrieval concepts.
- Semantic matching and discovery.
- Lightweight user experience focused on exploration.
- Source-oriented answer generation.
- Clear separation between retrieved knowledge and model-generated reasoning.

## Key Features

- Ask questions about content currently being viewed.
- Generate grounded answers from available information.
- Navigate information through conversation instead of manual searching.
- Support contextual exploration of documents and pages.
- Encourage source-aware answers.
- Surface relevant content through retrieval workflows.
- Support future citation and source-link experiences.
- Reduce friction between reading and understanding.

## Technical Challenges

One of the hardest problems is trust.

Users need confidence that answers are actually connected to the underlying content rather than being hallucinated by the model.

That requires careful retrieval, context selection, scope control, and answer generation.

Another challenge is knowing when not to answer.

A useful assistant should understand its boundaries. If the required information does not exist in the available content, the system should admit uncertainty rather than inventing a response.

Balancing helpfulness and reliability became one of the most interesting aspects of the project.

## Why It Matters

Ask Rudy represents a direction I find increasingly compelling: AI systems that help users understand information rather than simply generate information.

The project explores how retrieval, grounding, and contextual awareness can make AI more useful and trustworthy.

Conceptually, Ask Rudy also became one of the foundations for later experiments such as the Ask Anki experience inside this portfolio. Many of the same ideas appear in both systems:

- Retrieval before generation
- Grounded answers
- Clear scope boundaries
- Source-aware interactions
- Content-focused conversations

The broader idea is simple:

People should be able to talk to the information around them.

Ask Rudy explores what that future might look like.

chunks.title: Ask Rudy
chunks.text:
Title: Ask Rudy
Path: projects/ask-rudy.md
Section: What It Is

Ask Rudy explores a simple idea:

What if every page, document, note, or workspace could answer questions about itself?

Instead of acting as a general-purpose chatbot, Ask Rudy focuses on content-aware conversations. The assistant operates close to the information the user is currently viewing and attempts to answer questions using that context rather than relying entirely on model memory.

The project explores retrieval-based question answering experiences where users can interact with documents, articles, notes, saved content, browser pages, or personal knowledge collections through natural conversation.

Rather than asking users to manually search, skim, and piece together information, Ask Rudy attempts to act as a guide that helps users understand and navigate the content already available to them.

The goal is not to replace search. The goal is to make information easier to explore.

chunks.title: Ask Rudy
chunks.text:
Title: Ask Rudy
Path: projects/ask-rudy.md
Section: Why I Built It

Many AI assistants require users to leave the content they are working with and move into a separate chat interface.

That workflow always felt backwards.

Users are often reading a page, researching a topic, studying material, reviewing documentation, or exploring information when a question appears. The assistant should be able to help directly within that context.

Ask Rudy was built to explore that idea.

I wanted to investigate what happens when AI becomes a companion to existing content instead of acting as an isolated destination.

The project also allowed me to explore several themes that repeatedly appear throughout my work:

- Retrieval-based AI systems
- Browser-native experiences
- Grounded answers
- Local-first AI concepts
- Explainable AI interactions
- Human-friendly knowledge exploration

- Grounded responses based on available content.
- Browser-native interaction patterns.
- Context-aware conversational workflows.
- Embedding-driven retrieval concepts.
- Semantic matching and discovery.
- Lightweight user experience focused on exploration.
- Source-oriented answer generation.
- Clear separation between retrieved knowledge and model-generated reasoning.

chunks.title: Ask Rudy
chunks.text:
Title: Ask Rudy
Path: projects/ask-rudy.md
Section: Key Features

- Ask questions about content currently being viewed.
- Generate grounded answers from available information.
- Navigate information through conversation instead of manual searching.
- Support contextual exploration of documents and pages.
- Encourage source-aware answers.
- Surface relevant content through retrieval workflows.
- Support future citation and source-link experiences.
- Reduce friction between reading and understanding.

chunks.title: Ask Rudy
chunks.text:
Title: Ask Rudy
Path: projects/ask-rudy.md
Section: Technical Challenges

One of the hardest problems is trust.

Users need confidence that answers are actually connected to the underlying content rather than being hallucinated by the model.

That requires careful retrieval, context selection, scope control, and answer generation.

Another challenge is knowing when not to answer.

A useful assistant should understand its boundaries. If the required information does not exist in the available content, the system should admit uncertainty rather than inventing a response.

Balancing helpfulness and reliability became one of the most interesting aspects of the project.

chunks.title: Ask Rudy
chunks.text:
Title: Ask Rudy
Path: projects/ask-rudy.md
Section: Why It Matters

Ask Rudy represents a direction I find increasingly compelling: AI systems that help users understand information rather than simply generate information.

The project explores how retrieval, grounding, and contextual awareness can make AI more useful and trustworthy.

Conceptually, Ask Rudy also became one of the foundations for later experiments such as the Ask Anki experience inside this portfolio. Many of the same ideas appear in both systems:

- Retrieval before generation
- Grounded answers
- Clear scope boundaries
- Source-aware interactions
- Content-focused conversations

The broader idea is simple:

People should be able to talk to the information around them.

Ask Rudy explores what that future might look like.

documents.title: AstroValley
documents.summary: Browser-native AI sandbox disguised as an astrology application.
documents.elevatorPitch: Local-LLM experimentation platform using astrology as a structured sandbox for browser-native AI experiences.
documents.content:
# AstroValley

> A browser-native AI sandbox disguised as an astrology application.

## What It Is

AstroValley is a local-AI experimentation platform built around a deliberately unusual domain: astrology.

At first glance it appears to be an astrology product, but the real purpose is much broader. AstroValley serves as a sandbox for exploring local LLM orchestration, browser-native AI experiences, procedural world generation, semantic interactions, and rich visual interfaces that run close to the user.

The application uses astrology because it provides a highly structured data model containing planets, houses, signs, timelines, relationships, and symbolic meaning. That structure makes it an excellent environment for testing AI-driven experiences without starting from a blank slate.

The long-term vision is a personalized interactive world where data, visualization, AI reasoning, and procedural generation work together inside the browser.

## Why I Built It

I wanted a project that could combine several of the topics I had been exploring independently:

- Local LLM execution
- Browser runtimes
- AI orchestration
- Interactive 3D environments
- Deterministic world generation
- Rich visual storytelling

Astrology happened to provide a surprisingly useful framework because it contains structured entities, relationships, timelines, and interpretations that can be transformed into visual and conversational experiences.

Rather than building another chat application, I wanted to explore what AI feels like when it becomes part of a living environment.

## Technical Highlights

- Browser-native architecture.
- React + Next.js application foundation.
- React Three Fiber and Three.js rendering.
- WebGPU and WebLLM experimentation.
- Local Gemma model direction.
- astronomy-engine integration.
- Deterministic world generation.
- Persistent browser-side state.
- Seed-based procedural content generation.
- AI-assisted interaction layers built on structured domain data.

## Key Features

- Personalized valley generation.
- Deterministic worlds derived from user profile data.
- Procedural placement of houses and structures.
- Interactive planetary systems.
- Local-AI experimentation environment.
- Browser-side persistence.
- Rich 3D exploration experience.
- Visual representation of structured astrological data.

## Technical Challenges

One challenge is balancing playfulness with technical rigor. The experience should feel imaginative while still being built on deterministic systems that can be reproduced consistently.

Another challenge is local AI execution. Browser-side models introduce concerns around download size, GPU capabilities, memory limits, caching, and progressive loading.

Procedural generation creates a third challenge: generating worlds that feel unique while remaining deterministic and explainable.

## Why It Matters

AstroValley represents many of the themes that appear throughout my recent work.

It combines local AI, browser runtimes, procedural interfaces, structured data systems, and interactive visualization into a single platform.

The interesting part is not astrology itself. The interesting part is using a structured symbolic domain to explore what browser-native AI experiences might look like in the future.

documents.title: Crafticle
documents.summary: Authoring and publishing tools for structured creative writing.
documents.elevatorPitch: Human-in-the-loop AI writing workflows that transform rough ideas into structured articles.
documents.content:
# Crafticle

> An AI-assisted idea-to-article composer built around iterative human-AI collaboration.

## What It Is

Crafticle is an AI-assisted authoring environment focused on helping users transform rough ideas into structured, publishable content.

Rather than treating writing as a single prompt-and-response interaction, Crafticle treats writing as a workflow. Notes become themes. Themes become outlines. Outlines become sections. Sections become articles. The user remains in control throughout the process.

The project focuses on collaboration between the author and AI instead of complete automation.

## Why I Built It

Many AI writing tools optimize for speed. Enter a prompt, receive an article.

While that can be useful, I became more interested in the creative process itself. Most writing starts with incomplete thoughts, scattered notes, references, examples, and partially formed ideas.

Crafticle explores how AI can help organize, refine, expand, and improve those ideas without replacing the author's voice.

The goal is to make writing easier while preserving ownership and intent.

## Technical Highlights

- React-based authoring experience.
- TipTap editor integration.
- Structured idea extraction.
- Outline generation workflows.
- Content transformation pipelines.
- Human-in-the-loop editing.
- Iterative refinement workflows.
- Foundation for future publishing, templating, and content management capabilities.

## Key Features

- Convert rough notes into structured outlines.
- Generate article frameworks and section plans.
- Expand ideas through iterative refinement.
- Human-in-the-loop editing workflows.
- Rich text authoring with TipTap.
- AI-assisted drafting while preserving author intent.
- Foundation for publishing and content-management workflows.

## Technical Challenges

The hardest problem is preserving intent.

If the AI becomes too aggressive, the resulting article no longer feels like it belongs to the author. If the AI is too passive, the workflow provides little value.

Crafticle explores the balance between guidance and control, helping users move forward without taking ownership of the creative process away from them.

## Why It Matters

Crafticle reflects my broader interest in AI-native productivity tools.

The most interesting part of the project is not text generation itself. It is the workflow around generation: planning, refinement, organization, iteration, and collaboration.

That philosophy appears across many of my projects where AI acts as an accelerator for human work rather than a replacement for human decision-making.

documents.title: Florow
documents.summary: Adaptive spaced-repetition planning that turns a learning goal into a personalized review calendar.
documents.elevatorPitch: Enter a topic and a target date, and Florow generates an adaptive spaced-repetition plan that continuously adjusts based on your progress.
documents.content:
# Florow

> Adaptive spaced repetition planning for real-world learning goals.

## What It Is

Florow is a learning planner built around a simple idea:

Most spaced repetition tools optimize reviews after content already exists.

Florow starts one step earlier.

A learner enters a topic they want to learn and optionally provides a target date such as:

- Exam
- Certification
- Interview
- Presentation
- Personal milestone

Florow then generates a review schedule distributed across the available time window.

The result is a learning calendar that answers a simple question:

"What should I review today?"

## Why I Explored It

Traditional spaced repetition systems are excellent at retention but often require significant manual setup.

Users must create cards, manage decks, organize topics, and decide what to study.

I became interested in a planning-first approach.

What if learners could simply define a goal and a deadline?

Could the system automatically create a review plan and continuously adapt it as the learner progresses?

Florow emerged from that question.

## Technical Highlights

- Adaptive spaced repetition scheduling.
- Goal-driven learning plans.
- Calendar-based review visualization.
- Dynamic schedule adjustment.
- Progress-aware review density.
- Feedback-driven reinforcement.
- Learning workload balancing.
- Deadline-aware review planning.
- Retention-focused scheduling algorithms.

## Core Workflow

### Step 1: Define a Learning Goal

The learner provides:

Topic:
"System Design Interviews"

Target Date:
"September 15"

Optional inputs:

- Current familiarity
- Available study time
- Importance level

### Step 2: Generate Review Schedule

Florow creates a review calendar.

Example:

Week 1
- Introduction

Week 2
- First review

Week 4
- Reinforcement

Week 7
- Deep review

Week 10
- Final preparation

The schedule is distributed automatically across the available timeline.

### Step 3: Daily Reviews

The dashboard shows:

Today's Reviews

- System Design
- CAP Theorem
- Load Balancing

The learner focuses only on today's items.

### Step 4: Feedback Loop

After each review:

How did it go?

- Easy
- Good
- Difficult
- Need More Practice

The answer directly influences future scheduling.

## Adaptive Scheduling

If a learner reports:

"Easy"

Future review density decreases.

If a learner reports:

"Need More Practice"

Additional review sessions are inserted.

If a learner performs an unscheduled review early:

Future redundant review events can be removed automatically.

The calendar continuously reshapes itself around demonstrated understanding.

## Technical Challenges

The challenge is balancing retention with time.

Too many reviews create fatigue.

Too few reviews reduce retention.

The system must continuously answer:

- How much review is enough?
- How quickly should intervals expand?
- When should additional reviews be inserted?
- How should approaching deadlines affect scheduling?

The scheduling engine becomes the core product.

## Why It Matters

Learning is often treated as content management.

Florow treats learning as time management.

The learner already knows the goal:

Pass the exam.
Prepare for the interview.
Learn the subject.

The difficult part is consistently allocating review time.

Florow focuses on solving that scheduling problem.

## Long-Term Vision

The long-term vision is a personal learning planner that automatically manages retention over weeks and months.

Instead of manually deciding what to study every day, learners receive a continuously optimized review plan that adapts to their progress and target outcomes.

The goal is simple:

Spend less time planning what to learn and more time actually learning.

documents.title: Lintern
documents.summary: Local-first data guardrails for AI workflows.
documents.elevatorPitch: Local-first AI guardrails that detect sensitive information before it reaches external AI systems.
documents.content:
# Lintern

> Local-first data guardrails for AI workflows.

## What It Is

Lintern is a local-first data guardrail system designed for modern AI workflows. It started from a simple observation: people routinely paste internal information into AI tools without realizing how much context they are exposing. Customer information, source code, log files, internal project names, screenshots, support tickets, infrastructure details, and proprietary business information all become part of the prompt.

Lintern explores whether that risk can be reduced before the prompt is ever submitted.

The project combines browser-based inspection, sensitive-data detection, semantic analysis, and explainable feedback into a workflow that runs close to the user. Instead of routing content through another cloud service for inspection, the goal is to keep analysis local whenever possible.

The long-term vision is a privacy-first safety layer that can sit between users and AI systems, helping them understand what they are sharing and why it may matter.

## Why I Built It

Most discussions around AI safety focus on model behavior. I became interested in a different question: what happens before the model sees the prompt?

Enterprise users frequently interact with AI systems while working inside browsers, support tools, developer environments, CRMs, ticketing systems, documentation portals, and internal dashboards. In those environments, accidental disclosure is often a workflow problem rather than a model problem.

Lintern is my attempt to explore that space. The project investigates whether AI governance can become proactive, lightweight, and integrated into everyday work instead of existing only as policy documentation.

## Technical Highlights

- Local-first execution model.
- Browser-extension-friendly architecture.
- DOM-level inspection concepts.
- Sensitive data detection pipelines.
- Semantic matching and contextual classification.
- Explainable findings instead of opaque scores.
- Low-latency execution designed to feel native to user workflows.
- Architecture designed for future policy packs, custom detectors, redaction helpers, and organization-specific rules.

## Key Features

- Scan content before it is submitted to AI systems.
- Detect common sensitive-data patterns.
- Explore semantic detection beyond simple regex matching.
- Browser-extension-friendly workflow integration.
- Local-first processing and analysis.
- Explainable findings instead of black-box scores.
- Foundation for policy packs and organization-specific rules.

## Technical Challenges

The challenge is not detecting obvious items such as email addresses or phone numbers. The difficult part is identifying information that becomes sensitive because of context.

For example, a project codename, an internal customer reference, or a partial log snippet may not match traditional PII patterns yet may still be inappropriate to share externally.

Another challenge is user trust. A tool that flags too much becomes noise. A tool that misses important information becomes unreliable. The detection pipeline must balance recall, precision, transparency, and usability.

## Why It Matters

Lintern reflects several themes that appear throughout my work: local-first AI, browser-native tooling, explainable systems, and practical AI adoption.

More broadly, it explores a question I expect many organizations will face over the next decade:

How do we help people move faster with AI while maintaining privacy, governance, and operational safety?

chunks.text:
Title: Lintern
Path: projects/lintern.md
Section: What It Is

Lintern is a local-first data guardrail system designed for modern AI workflows. It started from a simple observation: people routinely paste internal information into AI tools without realizing how much context they are exposing. Customer information, source code, log files, internal project names, screenshots, support tickets, infrastructure details, and proprietary business information all become part of the prompt.

Lintern explores whether that risk can be reduced before the prompt is ever submitted.

The project combines browser-based inspection, sensitive-data detection, semantic analysis, and explainable feedback into a workflow that runs close to the user. Instead of routing content through another cloud service for inspection, the goal is to keep analysis local whenever possible.

The long-term vision is a privacy-first safety layer that can sit between users and AI systems, helping them understand what they are sharing and why it may matter.

chunks.title: Lintern
chunks.text:
Title: Lintern
Path: projects/lintern.md
Section: Why I Built It

Most discussions around AI safety focus on model behavior. I became interested in a different question: what happens before the model sees the prompt?

Enterprise users frequently interact with AI systems while working inside browsers, support tools, developer environments, CRMs, ticketing systems, documentation portals, and internal dashboards. In those environments, accidental disclosure is often a workflow problem rather than a model problem.

Lintern is my attempt to explore that space. The project investigates whether AI governance can become proactive, lightweight, and integrated into everyday work instead of existing only as policy documentation.

chunks.title: Lintern
chunks.text:
Title: Lintern
Path: projects/lintern.md
Section: Technical Challenges

The challenge is not detecting obvious items such as email addresses or phone numbers. The difficult part is identifying information that becomes sensitive because of context.

For example, a project codename, an internal customer reference, or a partial log snippet may not match traditional PII patterns yet may still be inappropriate to share externally.

Another challenge is user trust. A tool that flags too much becomes noise. A tool that misses important information becomes unreliable. The detection pipeline must balance recall, precision, transparency, and usability.

chunks.title: Lintern

Section: Why It Matters

Lintern reflects several themes that appear throughout my work: local-first AI, browser-native tooling, explainable systems, and practical AI adoption.

More broadly, it explores a question I expect many organizations will face over the next decade:

How do we help people move faster with AI while maintaining privacy, governance, and operational safety?

documents.summary: AI-powered learning trails that turn topics into navigable knowledge maps.
documents.elevatorPitch: AI-powered learning trails that turn topics into navigable maps of concepts and dependencies.
documents.content:
# OzMap

> AI-powered learning trails that turn topics into structured exploration maps.

## What It Is

OzMap is an AI-powered learning exploration platform that treats knowledge as a connected landscape rather than a collection of isolated pages.

A user starts with a topic and the system expands that topic into related concepts, dependencies, branches, prerequisites, and potential learning paths. Instead of repeatedly asking "what should I learn next?", the learner can visually navigate the subject and understand how ideas connect.

The project explores graph-oriented learning experiences where knowledge becomes something users can explore rather than simply search.

## Why I Built It

I have spent years thinking about learning systems, microlearning, educational tools, and knowledge organization.

Many AI systems are extremely good at generating explanations but provide very little guidance about where a learner should go next. The result is often a collection of disconnected answers.

OzMap explores a different model. Instead of generating a single response, the system attempts to generate a navigable structure.

The goal is not simply to answer questions. The goal is to help users build mental models.

## Technical Highlights

- Graph-based topic organization.
- AI-assisted topic expansion.
- Learning trail generation.
- Branching exploration paths.
- Dependency and prerequisite modeling.
- Interactive navigation and discovery.
- Structured progression through related concepts.
- Foundation for future quizzes, reviews, summaries, and learning recommendations.

## Key Features

- Generate learning maps from a starting topic.
- Visualize concept relationships and dependencies.
- Discover prerequisites and related concepts.
- Create branching learning trails.
- Support progressive exploration and knowledge discovery.
- Foundation for quizzes, summaries, and review workflows.

## Technical Challenges

Knowledge graphs generated by AI can grow extremely quickly. The challenge is maintaining meaningful structure while avoiding information overload.

Another challenge is granularity. If concepts are too broad, exploration feels shallow. If concepts are too detailed, navigation becomes overwhelming.

The system must continuously balance discovery, structure, and usability.

## Why It Matters

OzMap represents my broader interest in AI-native education systems and knowledge navigation.

The project explores how AI can become a guide through information rather than simply a generator of information.

That idea continues to influence many of my other projects involving learning systems, knowledge mapping, retrieval, and interactive exploration.

documents.title: Rudy Finds
documents.summary: Search-driven discovery experience built around semantic retrieval and AI-assisted exploration.
documents.elevatorPitch: Browser-native semantic search that helps users rediscover information using meaning instead of exact keywords.
documents.content:
# Rudy Finds

> A browser-native semantic search experiment focused on helping users rediscover information using meaning instead of exact keyword matching.

## What It Is

Rudy Finds explores a simple but surprisingly difficult problem:

People often remember what something meant, but they do not remember the exact words they used.

Traditional search systems are excellent when users know the right keyword, file name, page title, or phrase. The experience breaks down when users remember only the idea, topic, intent, or concept.

Rudy Finds investigates whether semantic retrieval can make discovery feel more natural by matching meaning instead of exact text.

The project explores browser-native search experiences where users can find relevant content from previously viewed pages, notes, documents, saved information, or personal knowledge collections using natural language. Rather than forcing users to remember where information lives, the system attempts to surface content based on conceptual similarity.

At its core, Rudy Finds is an experiment in helping users rediscover information they have already encountered but can no longer easily locate.

## Why I Built It

I kept noticing the same pattern in my own workflows.

I would remember reading something useful a few days earlier. I could remember the idea, the context, or the conclusion, but not the exact title or wording. Traditional search often failed because I was searching for meaning rather than keywords.

That observation led to a broader question:

What if search worked more like memory?

Instead of asking users to reconstruct the exact words used in a document, could a system help them find information based on concepts, relationships, and intent?

Rudy Finds was built to explore that idea.

The project also allowed me to investigate several themes that repeatedly appear throughout my work:

- Semantic retrieval
- Browser-native AI experiences
- Local-first discovery
- Knowledge navigation
- Human-centered search workflows
- Retrieval-augmented systems

## Technical Highlights

- Embedding-driven semantic retrieval concepts.
- Browser-native search workflows.
- Retrieval-focused user experience design.
- Natural language discovery patterns.
- Semantic similarity matching.
- Lightweight browser extension architecture.
- Exploration of local-first indexing and retrieval.
- AI-assisted content discovery.
- Foundation for future personal knowledge search experiences.

## Key Features

- Search using concepts instead of exact keywords.
- Discover relevant content through semantic matching.
- Explore browser-native retrieval experiences.
- Surface information based on intent and meaning.
- Reduce dependence on page titles and file names.
- Support natural language discovery workflows.
- Enable faster rediscovery of previously seen information.
- Provide a foundation for future AI-assisted search experiences.

## Technical Challenges

The most difficult challenge is balancing relevance and explainability.

A semantic search system may return results that are technically related but not obviously connected from the user's perspective. If users cannot understand why a result appeared, trust quickly disappears.

Another challenge is scope.

What content should be indexed? How much context should be stored? How can search remain useful while respecting privacy and maintaining fast response times?

The project explores these questions while investigating how retrieval systems can feel intuitive rather than mysterious.

## Why It Matters

Rudy Finds represents a direction I find increasingly important: helping people navigate information rather than simply generating more information.

The modern challenge is often not access to content. It is finding the right content at the right moment.

Semantic retrieval has the potential to make discovery feel more natural by allowing users to search using ideas rather than exact wording.

The project also connects closely to several other efforts in my portfolio, including Ask Rudy, Lintern, and local-first AI experiments. Together they explore how retrieval, semantic understanding, and browser-native experiences can help people work with information more effectively.

The broader vision is simple:

Search should feel less like querying a database and more like asking your memory for help.

Rudy Finds explores what that future might look like.

documents.summary: Multimodal storytelling system combining language, images, narration, and interaction.
documents.elevatorPitch: AI-powered storytelling platform that orchestrates text, images, and voice into a cohesive interactive experience.
documents.content:
# Storybook Arcade

> Once Upon a GPT / Storybook Arcade is a multimodal storytelling system that generates narrated, illustrated stories in real time.

## What It Is

Storybook Arcade explores what happens when AI storytelling moves beyond text generation.

The system coordinates multiple AI capabilities to create a complete storytelling experience. A story prompt becomes a narrative, the narrative becomes scenes, the scenes become images, the images become narration, and everything is combined into a synchronized experience.

Rather than focusing on a single model output, the project focuses on orchestration across multiple systems.

## Why I Built It

Most AI demonstrations stop after generating text.

I became more interested in what happens after generation. How do you transform a story into something that feels interactive, visual, and engaging?

Storybook Arcade was built to explore that question.

The project combines storytelling, image generation, voice synthesis, sequencing, and user interaction into a single creative workflow.

It also allowed me to experiment with physical and digital experiences working together.

## Technical Highlights

- LLM reasoning pipeline.
- Story planning and scene generation.
- Image generation orchestration.
- Text-to-speech integration.
- Streaming execution workflows.
- Multi-stage content generation.
- Coordinated multimodal output.
- Low-latency feedback loops.
- Physical and digital interaction concepts.

## Key Features

- Generate complete illustrated stories.
- Produce scene-by-scene visual content.
- Create synchronized narration.
- Coordinate text, images, and voice.
- Support interactive storytelling experiences.
- Real-time generation pipeline.
- Foundation for educational and creative storytelling systems.

## Technical Challenges

The hardest challenge was orchestration.

Language models, image generation systems, and text-to-speech engines all operate independently and have different performance characteristics, latency profiles, and output formats.

Creating a cohesive experience requires coordination, sequencing, error handling, and consistency across all generated content.

Maintaining story continuity across multiple generated scenes was another important challenge.

## Why It Matters

Storybook Arcade demonstrates one of the ideas that repeatedly appears throughout my work: the model itself is rarely the product.

The interesting engineering work often happens in the orchestration layer that coordinates multiple systems into a cohesive user experience.

This project showcases multimodal AI, workflow orchestration, interaction design, and the challenge of transforming raw AI capabilities into something people can actually use and enjoy.

## Demo

documents.summary: Runtime semantic capability coordination for agentic workflows.
documents.elevatorPitch: A conceptual runtime fabric that coordinates capabilities, policies, and intent paths for governed agentic systems.
documents.content:
# ZeroFabric

> Runtime semantic capability coordination for governed agentic systems.

## What It Is

ZeroFabric is a conceptual architecture for coordinating AI agents, services, applications, and business capabilities through a shared runtime fabric.

Most workflow systems today are built around predefined flows. Developers explicitly connect one component to another and define the sequence of execution ahead of time.

ZeroFabric explores a different model.

Instead of hardcoding workflows, participating systems expose capabilities, signals, constraints, policies, and abilities. A runtime fabric then determines which capabilities should participate in fulfilling a particular intent while remaining within governance boundaries.

The central idea is that systems should describe what they can do, while the runtime determines how those capabilities should be combined.

## Why I Explored It

As AI systems become more capable, the industry often swings between two extremes.

One extreme is rigid workflow automation, where every path is predefined.

The other extreme is unconstrained agent execution, where an LLM is allowed to dynamically decide everything.

Neither approach feels sufficient for large-scale enterprise systems.

I became interested in a middle ground where systems remain flexible and adaptive while still operating within clear constraints, approvals, policies, and observability boundaries.

ZeroFabric emerged from exploring what that middle layer might look like.

## Technical Highlights

- Runtime capability discovery.
- Semantic intent matching.
- Dynamic capability selection.
- Policy-driven execution.
- Approval-aware orchestration.
- Observable execution paths.
- Deterministic capability invocation.
- Governed agent collaboration.
- Event-driven coordination patterns.
- Runtime explainability and traceability.

## Key Concepts

### Orbs

An Orb represents a participating capability provider.

An Orb may expose:

- Signals
- Abilities
- Constraints
- Policies
- Metadata
- Approval requirements

Examples include:

- CRM systems
- Email systems
- Ticketing systems
- Payment systems
- Internal services
- AI agents

### Active Intent Paths

Rather than following a fixed workflow, the runtime constructs an execution path based on available capabilities and the current intent.

Example:

Signal A

→ Capability X

→ Capability Y

→ Capability Z

The path is selected dynamically but remains observable and explainable.

### Governance Layer

Every capability operates within defined constraints.

Examples include:

- Human approval requirements
- Spending limits
- Compliance policies
- Data-access restrictions
- Risk controls

The runtime cannot choose paths that violate governance rules.

## Technical Challenges

The hardest problem is balancing flexibility with predictability.

If the runtime is too rigid, it becomes another workflow engine.

If the runtime is too dynamic, it becomes difficult to reason about behavior, security, compliance, and reliability.

Another challenge is explainability.

Enterprise systems require clear answers to questions such as:

- Why was this path selected?
- Which capabilities participated?
- Which alternatives were considered?
- Which policies influenced the decision?

A successful runtime must remain inspectable and understandable.

## Why It Matters

I believe future enterprise AI systems will require an orchestration layer that sits between static workflows and unrestricted agents.

Organizations will need systems that can:

- Adapt to changing conditions.
- Discover capabilities dynamically.
- Operate within governance boundaries.
- Provide auditability and observability.
- Remain understandable to humans.

ZeroFabric is an exploration of that idea.

It is not a product implementation today. It is a conceptual framework for thinking about how governed agentic systems may be built in the future.

## Example Vision

Imagine a business environment where capabilities are published into a shared fabric:

- Email
- Calendar
- CRM
- ERP
- Support Systems
- AI Agents

A user expresses an intent.

The runtime evaluates available capabilities, policies, approvals, and constraints.

It then constructs an active intent path that fulfills the request while remaining compliant and observable.

That vision is the core motivation behind ZeroFabric.

Section: ZeroFabric

> Runtime semantic capability coordination for governed agentic systems.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: What It Is

ZeroFabric is a conceptual architecture for coordinating AI agents, services, applications, and business capabilities through a shared runtime fabric.

Most workflow systems today are built around predefined flows. Developers explicitly connect one component to another and define the sequence of execution ahead of time.

ZeroFabric explores a different model.

Instead of hardcoding workflows, participating systems expose capabilities, signals, constraints, policies, and abilities. A runtime fabric then determines which capabilities should participate in fulfilling a particular intent while remaining within governance boundaries.

The central idea is that systems should describe what they can do, while the runtime determines how those capabilities should be combined.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Why I Explored It

As AI systems become more capable, the industry often swings between two extremes.

One extreme is rigid workflow automation, where every path is predefined.

The other extreme is unconstrained agent execution, where an LLM is allowed to dynamically decide everything.

Neither approach feels sufficient for large-scale enterprise systems.

I became interested in a middle ground where systems remain flexible and adaptive while still operating within clear constraints, approvals, policies, and observability boundaries.

ZeroFabric emerged from exploring what that middle layer might look like.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Technical Highlights

- Runtime capability discovery.
- Semantic intent matching.
- Dynamic capability selection.
- Policy-driven execution.
- Approval-aware orchestration.
- Observable execution paths.
- Deterministic capability invocation.
- Governed agent collaboration.
- Event-driven coordination patterns.
- Runtime explainability and traceability.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Key Concepts

### Orbs

An Orb represents a participating capability provider.

An Orb may expose:

- Signals
- Abilities
- Constraints
- Policies
- Metadata
- Approval requirements

Examples include:

- CRM systems
- Email systems
- Ticketing systems
- Payment systems
- Internal services
- AI agents

### Active Intent Paths

Rather than following a fixed workflow, the runtime constructs an execution path based on available capabilities and the current intent.

Example:

Signal A

→ Capability X

→ Capability Y

→ Capability Z

The path is selected dynamically but remains observable and explainable.

### Governance Layer

Every capability operates within defined constraints.

Examples include:

- Human approval requirements
- Spending limits
- Compliance policies
- Data-access restrictions
- Risk controls

The runtime cannot choose paths that violate governance rules.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Technical Challenges

The hardest problem is balancing flexibility with predictability.

If the runtime is too rigid, it becomes another workflow engine.

If the runtime is too dynamic, it becomes difficult to reason about behavior, security, compliance, and reliability.

Another challenge is explainability.

Enterprise systems require clear answers to questions such as:

- Why was this path selected?
- Which capabilities participated?
- Which alternatives were considered?
- Which policies influenced the decision?

A successful runtime must remain inspectable and understandable.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Why It Matters

I believe future enterprise AI systems will require an orchestration layer that sits between static workflows and unrestricted agents.

Organizations will need systems that can:

- Adapt to changing conditions.
- Discover capabilities dynamically.
- Operate within governance boundaries.
- Provide auditability and observability.
- Remain understandable to humans.

ZeroFabric is an exploration of that idea.

It is not a product implementation today. It is a conceptual framework for thinking about how governed agentic systems may be built in the future.

chunks.title: ZeroFabric
chunks.text:
Title: ZeroFabric
Path: projects/zerofabric.md
Section: Example Vision

Imagine a business environment where capabilities are published into a shared fabric:

- Email
- Calendar
- CRM
- ERP
- Support Systems
- AI Agents

A user expresses an intent.

The runtime evaluates available capabilities, policies, approvals, and constraints.

It then constructs an active intent path that fulfills the request while remaining compliant and observable.

That vision is the core motivation behind ZeroFabric.
